questions.question: What percentage of GDP does India spend on Research and Development?
questions.explanation: India's Gross Expenditure on R&D as a percentage of GDP was 0.65% in 2020. This figure was reported by World Bank and UN data. The value had decreased from 0.66% in 2019. Indian government data also stated the GERD remained close to 0.64-0.66% during 2020-21. No significant change has been publicly reported since then.

questions.question: What is India’s approximate per capita R&D spending in USD?
questions.explanation: India’s Gross Expenditure on Research and Development (GERD) is about 0.64% of GDP according to the 2025-26 Economic Survey. India’s GDP is approximately $4.3 trillion USD in FY2025-26. Total GERD is around $27.5 billion USD. With a population of approximately 1.45 billion, per capita R&D spending is about 35 USD.

questions.question: Which agency received the highest research funding in Union Budget 2026-27?
questions.explanation: In the Union Budget 2026-27, DRDO received ₹29,100.25 crore for research and development. This allocation is higher than CSIR, ICMR, and DAE. DRDO’s allocation increased from ₹26,816.82 crore in FY 2025-26. DRDO operates under the Ministry of Defence. It covers defence research and next-generation technology projects. No other agency listed surpassed this allocation in the 2026-27 budget.

questions.question: Which industry had the highest R&D expenditure in 2025?
questions.explanation: The pharmaceutical industry spent the most on global R&D in 2025. Pharmaceutical R&D spending reached around $301 billion in 2023, sustained by major companies such as Bristol Myers Squibb ($14.8 billion), Johnson & Johnson ($13.6 billion), and AstraZeneca ($13.5 billion) in 2024. Drug development costs average $2.23 billion per asset, with continuous investment in clinical trials and new technologies.

questions.question: Which department gave maximum extramural R&D support in India in 2019-20?
questions.explanation: In 2019-20, the Department of Science and Technology contributed 55% of India’s total extramural R&D support. DST-funded projects accounted for Rs. 1,402.8 crore out of Rs. 2,529.42 crore approved. The academic sector received 70% of this extramural R&D support. DST and DBT were the two highest contributing departments to R&D support that year.
